# Agents in an Org Chart

## The mental model

The entities that do an organization's work — AI agents and humans alike —
are **roles on an org chart**. The moment you admit agents into the org chart
*as roles* — not as configs, scripts, or "models with slots" — you inherit
the entire apparatus humanity spent a century building to coordinate
specialized work: job design, complementary skills and personalities,
hiring, onboarding, SOPs, management, collaboration, performance review, and
promotion. An agent and an employee become **the same kind of thing for
governance purposes**: a *seat* with a mandate, equipped to do a job, placed
in a structure, managed, and held accountable.

Designing a seat is therefore not a parts-picking exercise. It is answering
one generative question: **"What would the best human in the world, in this
seat, in this organization, with this specific context and needs, actually
do?"** That question — not a catalog — generates the seat's skills, tools,
resources, SOPs, KPIs, rules, and judgment. You scope the role the way you'd
write a job posting, then equip it the way you'd hand a new hire their work
packet on day one.

## Why this earns its place

This Concept is the citation root for the entire role-birthing substrate. It
is cited by the six role-design Principles it founds (JOS-P25 through JOS-P30:
the best-human design question, the complete work packet, agent/human role
parity, world-standard research, archetype composition + onboarding roadmap,
and management as part of role design).

It also reconciles two processes JOS previously treated as unrelated: **agent
birthing** (the Foundry-Agent-System Context Pack + birthing phases) and
**human/venture onboarding** (JOS-SOP-009 + the context-bundle). Under this
Concept they are one act — birthing a role into an organization — which is why
it earns a place rather than living as an analogy inside JOS-C15.

It depends on existing physics as its read/write mechanism: JOS-C14 / JOS-C49 /
JOS-C51 (you can only assemble a work packet from a discoverable, composable
shelf) and JOS-C08 / JOS-C33 (you observe a role operating, then compress the
pattern into a reusable archetype). And it extends JOS-C15 (The Agent Line),
which decides *which* seats deserve to be persistent roles at all.

## What it IS (positive definition)

- **A seat is a role with a mandate**, filled interchangeably by a human or an
  agent. The governable unit is the seat, not the person or the model.
- **The design primitive is the best-human question.** "What would the best
  human in the world do in this seat, with this context?" generates the
  downstream specification (skills, tools, SOPs, KPIs, rules, judgment).
- **Birthing a seat mirrors the employee lifecycle:**

  | Hiring/HR step | Birthing equivalent |
  |---|---|
  | Job description / role design | charter + solve-for + domain & mandate |
  | Innate aptitude | model / archetype |
  | Onboarding work packet | tools + knowledge + data + credentials + channels + system-prompt (handbook) + SOPs + context (the why) |
  | KPIs / performance goals | success & learning metrics |
  | Rules, policies, culture | governance binds + trust ladder + escalation |
  | Org placement & management | reporting line, peers, comms topology, delegation |
  | Performance review / promotion | post-mortem + trust-ladder climb |

- **Archetypes are reusable job descriptions.** Roles cluster into families
  (Auditor, Analyst, Coordinator, Specialist, Warden…) that make the next hire
  cheap.
- **Collaboration is first-class.** Seats check and hand off to each other the
  way QA and continuous-improvement do in a real company; adversarial review
  is one management tool among many.

## What it is NOT

- **NOT a loose metaphor.** "Agents are kind of like employees" is decorative.
  This Concept asserts load-bearing *parity*: governance treats a seat the same
  whether a human or an agent fills it.
- **NOT a claim that every unit of work is a seat.** Flows, scripts, and
  one-shot calls are not roles — that boundary is JOS-C15's job. The org chart
  holds persistent roles, not ephemeral work.
- **NOT a directive.** The Concept observes; it does not command. The
  commitments live in the Principles it founds (JOS-P25–P30).
- **NOT rigid hierarchy.** "Org chart" means role/relationship structure
  (including peers, collaboration, and delegation), not a strict command tree.

## Worked examples

1. **Birthing an agent.** Instead of choosing graph nodes and tools from a
   catalog, you ask what the best human in that seat would do, write the role's
   charter, research its SOPs against the world standard, assemble its work
   packet (tools, knowledge, credentials, handbook, KPIs), place it under a
   manager, and give it an onboarding roadmap (probe → improve → tool-up). The
   Foundry-Agent-System "Context Pack" is this work packet in practice.

2. **Onboarding a human or a venture.** The same act: a context-bundle is the
   "why" half of a work packet; the role gets a mandate, resources, KPIs,
   reporting line, and a ramp. One substrate serves both human and agent hires.

3. **Adversarial review, reframed.** A Guardian seat and an Improver seat are
   not "two agents for adversarial review" — they are two complementary roles on
   the org chart that check each other the way QA and continuous-improvement do.
   Adversarial review is revealed as one management tool, not the whole of
   oversight.

## Connected Objects

- **[JOS-C15]** The Agent Line — which seats deserve to be persistent roles.
- **[JOS-C14]** Ambient Discoverability — work packets assemble from the shelf.
- **[JOS-C49]** Components as Parts on a Shelf — packet parts are shelf parts.
- **[JOS-C51]** Components Compose — typed composition assembles the packet.
- **[JOS-C08]** Compression Gradient — observed roles compress into archetypes.
- **[JOS-C33]** Visibility Before Compression — observe, then compress the role.
- **[JOS-C40]** Generative Absence — the empty seat generates its own spec.
- **foundation_for:** JOS-P25, JOS-P26, JOS-P27, JOS-P28, JOS-P29, JOS-P30.
